literal: the doctrine of non-onesidedness; from an-eka-anta ("not one-sided") + vāda ("doctrine")
invariant_definition: The Jaina doctrine of multifold predication: every existent has indefinitely many aspects, and any predicative statement about an existent is true only relative to a specified standpoint (*naya*). The doctrine commits the Jaina to (i) substance-modal (*dravya-paryāya*) ontology — every existent has both a persistent substantial aspect and a successive modal aspect; (ii) a finite seven-fold predication-schema (*sapta-bhaṅgī* / *syād-vāda*) that tracks which standpoint a given predication operates under; (iii) the rejection of any one *naya*'s claim to exhaustively describe an existent. The doctrine does NOT commit the Jaina to (a) relativism (contradictory predicates can never be predicated of the same subject under the same standpoint); (b) the assertion that contradictories are jointly true; (c) the modern liberal-pluralist 'all-positions-are-partially-correct' platitude. The Brahmanical refutations of *anekānta-vāda* (Bādarāyaṇa, Śaṅkara, Madhusūdana) generally argue that *anekānta* either collapses into self-contradiction (if contradictory predicates of the same subject are jointly asserted) or trivialises into innocuous pluralism (if standpoint-conditioned).

per_school.definition: *Anekānta-vāda* is articulated at *Tattvārtha-Sūtra* 1.34–35 in compressed form alongside the *naya*-doctrine. The seven *nayas* (*naigama*, *saṅgraha*, *vyavahāra*, *ṛjusūtra*, *śabda*, *samabhirūḍha*, *evambhūta*) give the standpoints under which predications are conditioned.

Akalaṅka's *Aṣṭa-Sahasrī* and *Siddhi-Viniścaya* develop the seven-fold *bhaṅga* predication and the *naya*-classification with full dialectical apparatus against Buddhist (Dharmakīrti-school) and Brahmanical opponents. The substance-modal (*dravya-paryāya*) account of reality is the metaphysical ground that allows the seven-fold predication to be coherent.

Malliṣeṇa's *Syādvāda-Mañjarī* gives the standard thirteenth-century exposition of *anekānta* as a meta-philosophical position covering all rival schools' positive theses; each rival fails by absolutising one *naya* and denying the others.

The *anekānta* thesis is refuted at *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.2.33–36 and in Śaṅkara's *bhāṣya* on the ground that contradictory predicates cannot belong to a single substance; Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi* engages the developed seven-fold *bhaṅga* predication and argues that it either collapses into self-contradiction or trivialises into the unobjectionable thesis that everything is somehow both A and not-A from different standpoints.

Madhva's *Tattva-Saṅkhyāna* and the anti-Jaina sections of his *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* engage *anekānta-vāda* as failing to give a determinate ontology; the *naya*-relativism is read as inconsistent with *pañca-bheda* realism.

translator_note: 'Multifold predication' or 'doctrine of manifold standpoints' acceptable. 'Relativism' is misleading — anekānta is not the thesis that all positions are equally valid but the thesis that every positive predication is conditioned by a determinate standpoint and that no single standpoint exhausts the existent. Western readers should not import the modern 'perspectivism' or 'pluralism' frame without qualification.
